1:
When Rehoboam came to Jerusalem after the disastrous conference at Shechem, he gathered the house of Judah and Benjamin — a hundred and fourscore thousand chosen warriors — to make war against Israel and bring the kingdom back to himself. The reformed John Gill treats this verse and the three that follow as matter already told in the older history, the same in substance as the account at 1 Kings 12:21, and is content to send the reader there. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sharpens the comparison: the chronicler deliberately omits the notice of 1 Kings 12:20, which had told of Jeroboam's call to the throne of Israel, and gives here only a slightly abridged form of 1 Kings 12:21; he marks even that the Hebrew word for "the kingdom" here differs from the term used in Kings.

His fellow grammatical-historical witness Keil and Delitzsch reads the verse as the head of a whole movement: the ten tribes having renounced their allegiance and made Jeroboam their king, the son of Solomon wished to compel them by force of arms again to submit, and for that purpose levied all the men capable of bearing arms in Judah and Benjamin — a design the prophet Shemaiah would presently forbid in the name of the LORD at v.4. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry weighs the muster morally. Rehoboam acts here "like a bold man," raising an army to reduce the revolters; and Henry notes why the two tribes held firm — Judah was the king's own tribe, which had owned him some years before the rest did, and Benjamin was the tribe in which the greater part of Jerusalem stood, which perhaps bound it to the house of David. Yet Henry will not praise the enterprise without warning: it is a dangerous thing to undertake anything, but especially a war, contrary to the will of God, and this expedition was determined for the sin of Solomon and could not prosper. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lays out the shape of the chapter that opens here — the array purposed against the ten tribes, the prophet's prohibition, the building of the cities of defence — and, like Gill, refers the details to the parallel at 1 Kings 12:21.

The witnesses do not divide. Ellicott and Gill fix the verse as the chronicler's compressed borrowing from the older record; Keil sets it at the head of Rehoboam's whole labour to consolidate his shrunken kingdom; Henry reads in the great levy both the natural resolve of a king to recover what he has lost and the hidden hand of God, who had decreed the division for Solomon's sin and would not suffer it to be undone by the sword. The hundred and eighty thousand are gathered; but the next verses will disband them, and the reign will be built not by the conquest of the north but by the fortifying of the south.
2:
"But the word of the LORD came to Shemaiah the man of God" — and the witnesses read the sentence as the true turning-point of the chapter, the moment a prophet's word halts a king's army. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take the verse as the opening of the oracle that runs through v.4: God commanded Rehoboam, in the name of the LORD and through Shemaiah, to desist from making war upon the Israelites, since they were his brethren; and the king abandoned his purpose, exactly as the parallel record relates at 1 Kings 12:22-24. It was this prohibition, they observe, that turned Rehoboam from the recovery of the north to the establishment of his dominion over Judah and Benjamin, so that the whole peaceable labour of the reign — the fortified cities, the gathered priests, the ordered household — hangs upon this single interposing word.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott brings his customary attention to the letter. Where the chronicler writes "the word of the LORD," the older account in Kings has "God" twice over; the chronicler, Ellicott notes, does not always avoid the covenant name Jehovah, and here restores it. And the prophet's name he gives in its fuller form, Shemayahu, where Kings has the shorter Shemaiah — the same lengthening the chronicler shows in writing Ahiyahu at 2 Chronicles 10:15. Small as these differences are, they are precisely the kind of trace by which the grammatical-historical eye follows the compiler's hand at work upon his source.

The title "the man of God" the witnesses let stand in its dignity. Shemaiah is no court flatterer but a bearer of the divine word, and the word he carries is not counsel but command: the levy of a hundred and eighty thousand men is to be dismissed at a syllable from heaven. That the message came to him unbidden — "the word of the LORD came" — marks it as revelation and not policy; the prophet does not seek the king, the word seeks the prophet, and through him seeks the king. So the two witnesses, though few, set the verse where it belongs in the argument of the chapter. Keil traces its effect through the whole reign; Ellicott secures its wording against the older text. Between them they show a kingdom's course altered not by the sword it had drawn but by the word it was made to hear — the God who had decreed the division for Solomon's sin now guarding that decree by the mouth of his prophet, and a king wise enough, this once, to obey.
3:
The oracle is addressed with a precision the witnesses find full of meaning — "to Rehoboam the son of Solomon, king of Judah, and to all Israel that is in Judah and Benjamin." The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott hears in the title "king of Judah" a quiet theological stroke: by this significant expression the accomplished facts of the division receive, at the very outset, the seal of divine assent. Rehoboam is not addressed as king of Israel, for Israel is his no longer; the LORD's own messenger styles him king of Judah, and so ratifies the loss the king had marched out to reverse.

The harder phrase, "all Israel in Judah and Benjamin," Ellicott turns over with care. The older account at 1 Kings 12:23 reads "all the house of Judah and Benjamin, and the remnant of the people," where the second clause seems to define the first — the house of Judah and Benjamin being just that remnant of Israel which continued loyal to David; on that reading the chronicler's phrase is a mere abbreviation, denoting whatever of Israel was comprised in the two faithful tribes. But there may be more, Ellicott suggests: a hint that Judah and Benjamin were now the true Israel, and that the apostate north had forfeited its right to the honourable name. Others, he allows, take the words of members of the northern tribes then dwelling within the southern territory. He records the variants too — the Syriac expanding the address, the Septuagint reducing it — the usual signs that the phrase was felt to be difficult.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley fastens instead on the patronymic, "the son of Solomon," and reads it as no idle formality. The naming intimates, he says, that this whole matter was determined for the sin of Solomon, and therefore could not be reversed — the very address carrying within it the reason the war must be abandoned. Rehoboam is bidden to remember whose son he is, and to see in the revolt not the insolence of subjects but the sentence of God upon his father's house.

The two witnesses do not divide. Ellicott secures the force of the titles and the fortunes of the text; Wesley draws the moral the titles imply. Between them the verse becomes more than a heading to a speech: it is a compressed theology of the division, naming Rehoboam king of the portion left him, marking the loyal tribes as the surviving Israel, and tracing the whole calamity back to the sin for which it was appointed and beyond recall.
4:
"Thus saith the LORD, Ye shall not go up, nor fight against your brethren: return every man to his house, for this thing is done of me." The command is total, and the witnesses weigh both its wording and the striking obedience it drew. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reports the words as given in the older account at 1 Kings 12:24, save that the chronicler omits "the children of Israel" after "your brethren." The decisive clause, "this thing is done of me," he renders more literally, "from me arose this matter" — the matter, that is, of the revolt of the ten tribes; and he sets it beside the chronicler's earlier confession at 2 Chronicles 10:15, "for the cause was of God." The revolt Rehoboam burned to crush was no mere accident of politics but the doing of the LORD, and to war against it was to war against heaven. Ellicott notes too that where Kings closes with the tautology "to go away according to the word of the LORD," the chronicler writes more cleanly "from going against Jeroboam," and that the versions preserve the usual singular "word" for his "words."

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the sentence to its issue: the people "obeyed the words of the LORD, and returned from going against Jeroboam." The great levy melts away at the prophet's word; a hundred and eighty thousand armed men turn home unblooded because God has forbidden the fight.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes that obedience the moral heart of the passage. Like a conscientious man, he says, Rehoboam let his design fall when God forbade it — whether because he reverenced the divine authority, or because he knew he should not prosper against a decree already gone forth. And though it looked mean, and would turn to their reproach among their neighbours, yet because God would have it so, they laid down their arms. Henry draws the warning plainly: it is a dangerous thing to undertake anything, but especially a war, contrary to the will of God; and even those destitute of true faith will at times be kept by his word from the wrong their nature is bent upon.

The witnesses converge on a single lesson. Ellicott and Keil fix the text and its fulfilment — the revolt owned as God's act, the army disbanded at his command; Henry reads in the disbanding the rare wisdom of submission. A word from a prophet has done what no counsellor could: it has taught a proud king that the kingdom is the LORD's to divide, and that the surest strength lies not in recovering what God has taken but in obeying the God who took it.
5:
With the war abandoned, the reign turns to defence, and the witnesses read the verse as the beginning of Rehoboam's real work — "And Rehoboam dwelt in Jerusalem, and built cities for defence in Judah." The reformed John Gill notes simply that the king dwelt in Jerusalem, the metropolis of Judah and the capital of his kingdom, making the royal city the centre from which the fortifying would radiate. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke supplies the strategy: Rehoboam was obliged to strengthen his frontiers against the encroachments of the men of Israel, and Jeroboam, for his part, did the very same thing to prevent the inroads of Judah, building up his own defences as recorded at 1 Kings 12:25. The divided kingdom braced itself along a new and hostile border.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses both the geography and the grammar. "Dwelt in Jerusalem" he takes to mean that Rehoboam kept it as his capital; "cities for defence" he renders "embattled cities," the Hebrew answering the Septuagint's fortified towns, the same construction that meets us at 2 Chronicles 8:5. And the phrase "in Judah," he insists, denotes not the tribal territory but the kingdom; for some of the fortresses in the list that follows lay within Benjamin, as the closing notice at v.10 will show. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley adds a needful qualification to the word "built": it does not mean that Rehoboam raised these cities from nothing, for they stood before; rather he repaired, enlarged, and fortified them, turning existing towns into strongholds.

The witnesses do not divide, and their several notes compose a single picture. Gill fixes the capital, Clarke the motive, Ellicott the sense of the terms and the reach of "Judah," Wesley the true force of "built." Between them they show a king who, forbidden to enlarge his borders by war, sets himself to secure the borders left him — dwelling at the centre in Jerusalem and ringing his shrunken realm with repaired and embattled cities. What might have looked like defeat is turned to prudence: the same energy that would have been spent reducing the north is now spent making the south defensible. And the qualification Wesley adds is itself instructive, for it marks the peaceable character of the work — not the founding of new towns for ambition's sake, but the strengthening of old ones for safety, the wisdom of a ruler who has learned to make the best of what God has left him rather than to grasp after what God has taken away.
6:
The naming of the first fortresses begins at Bethlehem, and the witnesses linger over the little town whose fame far outran its size. The reformed John Gill marks it at once as "the place of our Lord's birth," about six miles from Jerusalem. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill in its pedigree: Bethlehem lay in Judah, a small city mentioned as early as Jacob's time at Genesis 35:19, two hours south of Jerusalem, the birthplace of David and of Christ, foretold at Micah 5:2 and fulfilled at Matthew 2:5, and known in their day as Beit-Lahm. His fellow grammatical-historical witness Charles Ellicott sets it on its rocky eminence and gathers the same testimonies to David's city and the Messiah's, at Genesis 35:19, Micah 5:2, and Matthew 2:6.

On the second and third names the two grammatical-historical witnesses turn to the geographer's work. Etam, both are careful to say, is not the place of the same name that meets us at 1 Chronicles 4:32 and in the story of Samson at Judges 15:8, which lay in Simeonite territory; Ellicott identifies Rehoboam's Etam with Ain Attan, and Keil, weighing the rival guesses of the explorers, prefers the same valley south-west of the pools of Solomon to the identification with the present village of Urtas that Robinson had maintained, finding the name Ain Attan to answer better to the ancient Etam. Tekoa, Ellicott notes, survives as the ruins of Teku'a on a hill two hours south of Bethlehem, the same site the older records fix at Joshua 15:59; both witnesses set it in the same barren highland where a shepherd of a later age would be called to prophesy. Ellicott adds the small but pointed observation that "he built" here means, as throughout the list, not that he founded but that he fortified.

The witnesses do not divide; they simply share the labour of identification. Gill and the two grammatical-historical voices agree that these were real and locatable towns, strung along the high country south of Jerusalem, and that the chronicler's "building" is the fortifying of a defensive line. Yet the reader can hardly pass Bethlehem without pausing where the witnesses pause: the first of Rehoboam's strongholds is the town that would one day cradle great David's greater Son. Rehoboam fortified it for the fears of his own hour, walling a frontier against Jeroboam and Egypt; but the same little city he braced for war would be remembered not for any siege it withstood, but for the birth that made it, of all the towns in this martial catalogue, the one whose name the whole world would learn.
7:
The list moves on to Bethzur, Shocho, and Adullam, and the witnesses trace each to its site. The reformed John Gill notes only that all three lay in the tribe of Judah. The grammatical-historical witnesses supply the topography. Keil and Delitzsch place Beth-zur where the ruin of that name then stood, midway between the pools of Solomon and Hebron, the site fixed at Joshua 15:58; Shoko they set at the present Shuweike in the Wady Sumt, some three and a half hours south-west of Jerusalem, as at Joshua 15:35; and Adullam, reckoned in Joshua 15:35 among the cities of the lowland, they confess had not yet been certainly discovered, weighing Tobler's conjecture of Dula against the likelier Deir Dubban north of Beit-Jibrin, near the site named at Joshua 12:15.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, confirms the identifications and refines them. Beth-zur he gives as Beit-sur, again midway between the pools and Hebron; Shoco as es Suweikeh in the Wady Sumt, the town near which David faced Goliath at 1 Samuel 17:1; and Adullam, its exact site still uncertain, he would place perhaps at Aid-el-Mieh, pointing back for its earlier mention to Joshua 15:35. The two grammatical-historical voices thus agree on the first two and hold the third an open question, a candour characteristic of their method — where the ground has not yielded up a name, they say so, rather than force a false certainty.

The witnesses do not contend, and the reader who follows their maps sees the strategy taking shape. These were no random towns but a chain of strongholds along the western and southern approaches to the heart of Judah, guarding the roads by which an enemy — whether Jeroboam from the north or, more ominously, Egypt from the south — might march on Jerusalem. Adullam especially carried an old resonance, for its cave had once sheltered the outlawed David and his men; the city that had hidden the fugitive king now became a bastion of his grandson's kingdom. Gill anchors all three in Judah; Keil and Ellicott set them on the land and confess the limits of their knowledge. Between them they turn a bare roll of names into a visible line of defence, and remind us that behind the theology of the chapter — the kingdom preserved for the covenant's sake — lay the hard and practical labour of walls and gates raised on real hills of a real land.
8:
Gath heads the next group, and its name at once raises a question, for the great Gath was a Philistine capital. The reformed John Gill judges that this cannot be that Gath which was one of the five Philistine principalities but a city of Judah, otherwise unmentioned, unless it be the same as Moresheth-gath. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch keep to the famous city, the royal seat of the Philistines first made subject to Israel by David at 1 Chronicles 18:1, and under Solomon the seat of a vassal king as at 1 Kings 2:39, though its exact site, they admit, had not been certainly discovered. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, likewise leaves Gath uncertain, proposing perhaps the Wady-el-Gat north of Ascalon, and draws from the comparison of 1 Kings 2:39 with 1 Chronicles 18:1 the same inference, that under Solomon Gath was ruled by a king subject to Israel.

On Mareshah the two grammatical-historical witnesses agree closely. Keil sets it on the road from Hebron to the Philistine country, the later Marissa, marked by the ruin Marash near Beit-Jibrin, the Eleutheropolis of the Greeks, its earlier mention at Joshua 15:44; Ellicott gives the same ruin south of Beit-Jibrin and adds its later fame as the field of Asa's great battle at 2 Chronicles 14:9. Ziph, Ellicott fixes at Tel Zif, the ruins about an hour and a quarter south-east of Hebron, in the wilderness where David hid from Saul at 1 Samuel 23:14, and carefully distinguishes it from another Judean Ziph named at Joshua 15:24.

So a mild difference stands over the first name — whether Rehoboam's Gath was the old Philistine capital, now a subject city drawn into his defences, or a lesser Judean town of the same name — while over Mareshah and Ziph the witnesses are one. The disagreement is not idle: if the great Gath is meant, then Rehoboam's line of forts reached out to hold a former Philistine stronghold on the coastal approach, a telling sign of how far south and west his fears looked. The witnesses divide the labour as before. Gill guards the improbability of a Philistine capital in a list of Judean forts; Keil and Ellicott keep the famous city and its royal history, and settle Mareshah and Ziph on ground made familiar by the wanderings of David. The land itself, in their reading, is thick with memory: the very hills Rehoboam now walled had sheltered his great ancestor when he too was hunted through the wilderness of Judah.
9:
Three more names complete the southern reach of the list — Adoraim, Lachish, and Azekah — and the witnesses attend to their sites and their later fortunes. The reformed John Gill identifies Adoraim with the Adora that Josephus names, mentioned by him alongside Mareshah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch enlarge the history: Adoraim, the Adora of Josephus, became in later ages an Idumean city, taken by Hyrcanus and afterwards rebuilt by Gabinius under the name Dora, and Robinson had identified it with the present Dura, a village some seven and a half miles west of Hebron. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, gives the same Dura south-west of Hebron and observes that the name occurs nowhere else in the Old Testament, though it is often met in Josephus in connection with Marissa.

Lachish the two grammatical-historical witnesses place together in the lowland of Judah. Keil sets it at Um Lakis on the road from Gaza to Beit-Jibrin and Hebron, a circular height covered with ancient walls and marble fragments, its earlier notice at Joshua 10:3 and Joshua 15:39; Ellicott names the same ruined city on its round hill west of Beit-Jibrin. Azekah both leave less certain: Keil places it near Shoco and, by the witness of 1 Samuel 17:1, near Ephes-dammim, while Ellicott simply calls its site uncertain, near Socoh, citing the same battlefield at 1 Samuel 17:1 and the pursuit of the kings at Joshua 10:10.

The witnesses do not divide; they gather the same towns onto the same map and confess the same uncertainty where the ground is silent. And the reader who marks their identifications sees the design of the whole line grow plainer with every name. Lachish especially was a great fortress of the Judean lowland, one that later ages would see besieged by Assyrian and Babylonian armies; Azekah stood in the valley of Elah where David felled Goliath. Rehoboam's chain of strongholds ran through country soaked in the memory of Israel's older battles and pointed, by its very reach toward the south-west, at the quarter from which the gravest danger would come. Gill fixes Adoraim by Josephus; Keil and Ellicott set all three on the land and trace their after-history through the wars of later centuries. Between them they show the chronicler's dry list to be, when unfolded, the map of a kingdom bracing its most exposed flank — the flank that, in the very next chapter, Shishak of Egypt would overrun.
10:
The list closes with Zorah, Aijalon, and Hebron, and with the summary note that these fenced cities lay "in Judah and in Benjamin." The reformed John Gill glosses only the first, Zorah, as the same place elsewhere called Zoreah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify Zorah with the ruin Sura, Samson's birthplace, on the ridge above the Wady es Surar, as at Joshua 15:33; Aijalon with the village Jalo on the edge of its plain, four leagues west of Gibeon, named at Joshua 10:12 and Joshua 19:42; and Hebron, the ancient city of the patriarchs, then called el Khalil, "the friend of God," after Abraham, as at Genesis 23:2. All these, they note, lay in Judah save Zorah and Aijalon, which had been assigned to Dan at Joshua 19:41 and were probably taken over by Benjamin after part of the Danites emigrated northward, as told at Judges 18:1 — which is why they can here be reckoned among the cities of Benjamin.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, agrees at every point: Zorah the birthplace of Samson, Aijalon its neighbour, the two of them the farthest north of all the fifteen forts, become Benjamite at the migration of Dan; Hebron the patriarchs' city. He fixes the force of the closing clause — "which are in Judah and in Benjamin" refers to the entire list, not the last names only — and marks the phrase "fenced cities" as one peculiar to the chronicler, recurring at 2 Chronicles 12:4. Above all he draws, from the critic Bertheau whom he cites, the strategic conclusion the whole map implies: the fifteen cities, Zorah and Aijalon excepted, lay on the south and west of Jerusalem, so that Rehoboam appears to have been more afraid of an attack from the south and west — that is, from Egypt — than of a war with the northern kingdom.

The witnesses do not divide, and Ellicott's closing point gathers the meaning of the entire catalogue. What has looked like a mere gazetteer proves to be a confession of where the king's fears truly lay. Gill and the two grammatical-historical voices set the last towns on the land; Bertheau, through Ellicott, reads the pattern of the whole. The line of forts bends away from Jeroboam and toward the Nile, and the reader who has followed the names from Bethlehem to Hebron sees that Rehoboam, forbidden to march north, spent his strength guarding the south — a foresight the next chapter would prove neither idle nor, in the end, sufficient, when Shishak came up against the fenced cities of Judah and took them.
11:
"And he fortified the strong holds, and put captains in them, and store of victual, and of oil and wine." The witnesses read the verse as the inward completion of the outward walls — a fortress is only as strong as its garrison and its granary. The reformed John Gill takes it plainly of the fortifying of these and the other cities. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch unfold the several measures: Rehoboam increased the strength of the fortresses by putting them in thorough condition to withstand attack, appointing commandants — the Hebrew negidim — provisioning them, and, as the next verse adds, laying up stores of arms, so that he made them exceedingly strong. They read the closing clause of the following verse, "and he had Judah and Benjamin," as answering the notice that Israel had revolted from the house of David at 2 Chronicles 10:19, and so as rounding off the whole account of what Rehoboam did to consolidate his kingdom.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens on the "store of victual," and sees in it a double prudence: in these places the king laid up stores of provisions, not only to enable the cities to endure a siege, but also, from their position, to supply desolate districts in time of need. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, sharpens the vocabulary: "fortified" means literally "strengthened," put into efficient condition for defence; the "strong holds" are the fortresses of the previous verse; and the "captains," the negidim, are leaders or princes, the same word used at 1 Chronicles 9:11, meaning here commandants set over the garrisons.

The witnesses do not divide; they simply itemize a single wise provision. Gill fixes the fact, Keil the full range of the measures, Clarke the foresight of the stores, Ellicott the precise sense of the terms. Between them they show a king who understood that fortification is more than masonry: walls without men to hold them, and men without bread and oil and wine to sustain them, are no defence at all. Rehoboam appoints his officers, fills his storehouses, and readies each city to stand alone through a siege — and even to feed the country around it. It is the picture of a ruler making, in peace, the sober preparations that war will one day test; and though the chronicler's larger point is that the kingdom's true strength lay in the covenant and in the gathered worshippers of the LORD, he does not despise the ordinary means of prudence, but records them as the diligent labour of a king setting his house in order against the day of trouble.
12:
"And in every several city he put shields and spears, and made them exceeding strong, having Judah and Benjamin on his side." With this the account of Rehoboam's defences reaches its close, and the witnesses weigh the armament and the phrase that seals it. The reformed John Gill notes that the king furnished the cities with shields and spears — these named for all the weapons of war, both to defend the garrisons and to annoy an attacking foe. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read the stocking of arms as the last of the measures by which Rehoboam made the fortresses exceeding strong, and take the closing words, "and he had Judah and Benjamin," as the formal conclusion of the whole section on the establishing of his power, the counterpart to the revolt of Israel recorded earlier.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, brings his usual care to the wording. The "shields," he observes, are the "targets" of 2 Chronicles 9:15; "made them exceeding strong" he renders "strengthened them very abundantly," a form of words he notes is used only by the chronicler, recurring at 2 Chronicles 16:8 and in the like construction at Nehemiah 6:18. The last clause, "having Judah and Benjamin on his side," he reads literally as "and there fell to him Judah and Benjamin" — a definition of Rehoboam's territory, echoing the account of the two loyal tribes at 2 Chronicles 10:17.

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement lets the verse do its summarizing work. Gill fixes the arms and their purpose; Keil marks the clause as the seal of the whole record of fortification; Ellicott secures the idiom and the sense of the territorial notice. Between them they close the martial half of the chapter on a note of settled strength: every city walled, garrisoned, provisioned, and now armed, and behind them the solid loyalty of the two southern tribes. The king who had been forbidden to enlarge his realm has made the realm he keeps secure. Yet the phrasing the witnesses recover carries its own quiet theology — "there fell to him Judah and Benjamin" — for the two tribes were less won by Rehoboam's policy than left to him by the LORD's own decree, the portion appointed to the house of David when the rest was torn away. The shields and spears are his doing; the people behind them are God's gift. And it is to that gift's deeper enrichment — the priests and Levites and true worshippers who now flock to Judah — that the chapter next turns.
13:
The chapter turns from walls to worshippers, and the witnesses greet the migration of the priests and Levites as the true strengthening of the kingdom. The reformed John Gill notes that these were the priests and Levites who had been settled in all Israel — in the cities assigned them throughout the ten tribes — and who now resorted to Rehoboam out of all their coasts. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain the verb: "to present oneself before" another, to place oneself at his disposal, as at Job 1:6; the Levites, that is, went over to Rehoboam, and the motive of their going will be given in the verse that follows — that Jeroboam had erected his own worship at Bethel and Dan, set up the calves, and appointed to his altars not the sons of Levi but men drawn from the body of the people.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the migration into a meditation on faithfulness under persecution. Rehoboam, he says, was strengthened by the accession of the priests and Levites, and of all the devout and pious Israelites who were true to their God and their religion; where God's faithful priests are, there his faithful people should be, and it was the wisdom and the praise of Rehoboam and his people that they bade the refugees welcome, though they crowded themselves to make room. Conscientious refugees, Henry adds, bring a blessing with them to the countries that entertain them, as they leave a curse behind them with those that expel them; it was a mercy that these outcasts had a place to flee to, and an evidence that they loved their work better than their maintenance. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, confirms the sense of the words: "resorted to him" means they presented themselves before him, as at Job 2:1; "coasts" is their border or domain; and "Levites" is here used in the general sense that includes the priests.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill and Ellicott fix who came and from where; Keil supplies the reason they will presently learn; Henry reads in the whole exodus a lesson on the fellowship of the faithful. Between them they show the chronicler's deepest point: that Rehoboam's kingdom was fortified less by shields and spears than by the influx of men who would rather lose their homes than their altar. The strength that mattered was not the garrisons but the gathered servants of the LORD, driven south by a king who had cast off God's ministers, and welcomed by a king wise enough to take them in. It is the beginning of that spiritual reinforcement which, the chapter will say, made Judah strong for three good years.
14:
The verse tells why the Levites came — "for the Levites left their suburbs and their possession, and came to Judah and Jerusalem, for Jeroboam and his sons had cast them off from executing the priest's office unto the LORD." The reformed John Gill notes the cost of their coming: they left the suburbs and fields adjacent to the cities given them throughout the tribes, the forty-eight Levitical cities, and came to those of Judah and to Jerusalem where they might exercise their office. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott glosses the loss precisely — the "suburbs" are the pasture-grounds appointed at Numbers 35:1-8, and the "possession" the cities assigned them among the ten tribes — and explains the casting off from 1 Kings 12:26-31: Jeroboam, to keep his subjects from the temple, set up two centres of worship of his own, and in staffing them with priests taken promiscuously from all classes struck a direct blow at the Levitical order, thrusting the true priests out from acting as priests to Jehovah.

On the phrase "and his sons" Ellicott and Keil agree that it commonly means Jeroboam's successors, since, as Keil observes, all the kings of Israel walked in his footsteps; only one of Jeroboam's own sons, Nadab, actually reigned, as at 1 Kings 15:25. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the expulsion as deliberate policy: Jeroboam drove out the priests and Levites lest they turn the hearts of the people to the true God, and so lead them to revolt back to Judah, as he feared at 1 Kings 12:26; and therefore he established a new worship and made new gods. John Wesley, of the same school, weighs both sides of the breach: Jeroboam would not suffer the Levites to instruct the Israelites in God's worship, nor to go up to Jerusalem in their courses; and they, for their part, would not join in the worship of the calves as they were commanded, and so willingly forsook all their patrimonies and possessions for God's sake. "No secular advantages whatsoever," Wesley urges, "should detain us there, where we are in danger of making shipwreck of faith and a good conscience."

The witnesses do not divide. Gill and Ellicott reckon what the Levites gave up; Clarke and Keil expose Jeroboam's motive; Wesley draws the lesson of a costly fidelity. Between them they portray a clean and painful choice: the ministers of the LORD, offered the keeping of their homes on condition of serving the calves, chose exile with a good conscience over comfort with a corrupted one. What Jeroboam meant as a purge of his kingdom became the enriching of Rehoboam's, and the chapter counts as pure gain to Judah what the north had cast away.
15:
"And he ordained him priests for the high places, and for the devils, and for the calves which he had made." The witnesses turn to the counterfeit priesthood and the objects of its worship, and they read the verse as the measure of Jeroboam's apostasy. The reformed John Gill notes only that he ordained priests for the high places out of men who were not of the tribe of Levi nor of the family of Aaron. The grammatical-historical witnesses press the strange word "devils." Keil and Delitzsch take the demons so named from the Egyptian idolatry in which the worship of the goat, of Pan or Mendes, held a prominent place, the term drawn from Leviticus 17:7; Charles Ellicott renders them "satyrs," the goat-shaped demons of Isaiah 13:21, literally "goats," and finds in the word a mark of how debasing the idolatry of Jeroboam's day had become.

On the calves themselves a real difference of emphasis appears. Ellicott insists that the calves "represented the God of Israel," so that Jeroboam encouraged a system of syncretism, a mixture of worships — Jehovah served under the image of the ox even as the goat-demons were courted beside him. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reads the images more as sheer heathenism: the devils were the hairy ones, probably goats worshipped after the Egyptian manner, and the golden calves themselves were in imitation of the Egyptian ox-god Apis. John Wesley, of the same school, distinguishes two sorts of high places at once — some for the devils, the Baals or false gods which many of Jeroboam's people worshipped and which he gave them liberty to serve, and some for the calves, by and in which he pretended to worship the true God. So the witnesses circle the same dark scene from slightly different angles: was the calf-worship a corrupt cult of the true God, a syncretism, or a plain lapse into Egyptian idolatry?

The difference is one of emphasis, not of verdict, for none of the witnesses doubts that the whole system was abominable. Gill fixes the illegitimacy of the priesthood; Keil and Ellicott expose the goat-demons behind the "devils"; Ellicott and Wesley have the calves masking the God of Israel, while Clarke traces them to Egypt. Between them they lay bare the thing the Levites fled: an altar served by men of no priestly line, ringed with the worship of goat-demons, and centred on golden calves that, whether meant for Jehovah or for Apis, had turned the worship of Israel into a lie. It is against this that the faithful priests, and after them the faithful people, voted with their feet — and their flight is the chapter's true measure of Jeroboam's sin.
16:
"And after them out of all the tribes of Israel such as set their hearts to seek the LORD God of Israel came to Jerusalem, to sacrifice unto the LORD God of their fathers." The migration widens now from clergy to laity, and the witnesses mark the movement of the devout. The reformed John Gill notes that besides the priests and Levites, and after their example, many of the common people out of all the ten tribes came south. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch explain that "after them" means following the priests and Levites, and that those who "set their hearts" — the same idiom as at 1 Chronicles 22:19 — went to Jerusalem not merely to offer sacrifice but, as the sequel shows, to remain in the kingdom of Judah. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, confirms the phrase as peculiar to the chronicler, again at 1 Chronicles 22:19, and reads "came to sacrifice" as implying that they came also to settle, comparing the like migrations of the faithful in the reigns of Asa at 2 Chronicles 15:9 and Hezekiah at 2 Chronicles 30:11.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke measures the extent of the influx: all the truly pious joined Rehoboam out of every tribe, and the whole tribe of Levi, deprived of its functions, came over, so that he had Judah, Benjamin, and Levi, and probably a part of Simeon — for he held Etam, which lay in Simeonite territory — together with the godly out of all the other tribes, who could not bear Jeroboam's idolatry. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads their choice as the pattern of all right priorities: such as set their hearts to seek the LORD, making conscience of their duty, left the inheritance of their fathers and took houses in Jerusalem, that they might have free access to the altar of God and be out of the temptation to worship the calves. "That is best for us which is best for our souls," Henry writes, "and in all our choices advantages for religion must take place of all outward conveniences."

The witnesses do not divide. Gill and Clarke reckon how many and from where; Keil and Ellicott show that they came not to visit but to stay; Henry draws the abiding lesson of their sacrifice. Between them they portray a quiet, costly exodus of the faithful — men who valued the pure worship of God above the fields and homes of their fathers, and who would rather be strangers in Jerusalem with a free altar than settled in the north with a corrupted one. Their coming, following the priests they honoured, is the chapter's proof that when a state casts off true religion, true religion does not perish but removes, carrying its blessing to the place that will receive it.
17:
"So they strengthened the kingdom of Judah, and made Rehoboam the son of Solomon strong, three years." The witnesses gather around both the source of that strength and the limit of its duration. The reformed John Gill notes plainly that the immigrants made the kingdom more numerous and better able to defend itself. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch look deeper: the priests, Levites, and pious worshippers strengthened Judah by strengthening the religious foundation on which the kingdom rested, and made Rehoboam strong for three years, during which king and people walked in the way of David and Solomon; the strengthening lasted only so long as the opposition to Jeroboam's innovations was kept alive by the steady emigration of the godly, and what befell after those three years is told in the chapter that follows at 2 Chronicles 12.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fixes the same span: for three years Rehoboam walked in the way of David and prospered, but for fourteen years after he and his people were unfaithful to the LORD, save at such intervals as his judgments were upon them. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads the three years as a covenant of blessing: by their piety and their prayers the faithful procured a blessing on the kingdom that was a sanctuary to them, and made Rehoboam strong just so long as they walked in the good way; but when that was forsaken, and the king threw himself out of God's favour, the best friends he had could no longer strengthen him — "we retain our strength while we cleave to God and our duty, and no longer."

Over the last clause, "the way of David and Solomon," a fine difference of reading opens. John Wesley, of the wesleyan-arminian school, takes the honourable naming of Solomon as a pattern of piety to be a considerable evidence of his true repentance before his death — the sacred writer would not else have set him beside David as a model. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, reads it the other way: the chronicler, true to his manner, simply ignores here the foreign religions Solomon countenanced in his old age, and holds up only the better part of his reign; and Ellicott adds, from the critic Bertheau whom he cites, that there is no ground to suspect the antiquity of the record, since the pressure from the north would naturally quicken the religious earnestness of the south. So the witnesses agree on the fact — three good years founded on true worship — while differing on what the praise of Solomon implies: for Wesley a token of the old king's penitence, for Ellicott a token of the chronicler's selective art. Gill and Clarke fix the span, Keil and Henry its cause and its lesson, and the chapter is left poised on the edge of the decline that the next will record.
18:
The chapter turns to Rehoboam's household, and the witnesses begin with a tangle over his first-named wife and her parentage. The reformed John Gill observes that of this Jerimoth, called a son of David, we read nowhere else; perhaps he was the son of one of David's concubines, or bore two names. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch prefer, with the Hebrew margin, the many manuscripts, and the ancient versions, to read "the daughter" of Jerimoth rather than "the son," and note that no Jerimoth appears among David's sons at 1 Chronicles 3:1-8 — unless the name be a variant of Ithream at 1 Chronicles 3:3 — so that he was likely a son of a concubine. They further supply a dropped conjunction before Abihail, so that the verse names not one parent but two: Mahalath was the daughter of Jerimoth the son of David and of Abihail the daughter of Eliab, David's eldest brother, named at 1 Samuel 17:13 and 1 Chronicles 2:13.

Charles Ellicott, of the same school, agrees on both points, reading "the daughter" with the Septuagint and Vulgate and taking Jerimoth for one of the sons of the concubines at 1 Chronicles 3:9. But here a genuine question divides the tradition. The Septuagint and Vulgate make Abihail a second wife of Rehoboam; Ellicott and Keil deny it, arguing from the singular verbs of the following verses — "she bare" at v.19 and "after her" at v.20 — that only one wife is meant, and that a daughter of David's eldest brother could hardly have become the wife of David's grandson. Abihail, then, is Mahalath's mother, not Rehoboam's bride.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke passes over the philology to the policy: by taking Mahalath, and so marrying within the family of David, Rehoboam strengthened his own right to the throne. The witnesses do not, in the end, divide over the substance. Gill, Keil, and Ellicott agree that the received "son" should be "daughter" and that Jerimoth was likely a concubine's child; Keil and Ellicott settle, against the older versions, that Abihail is the wife's mother and not a second wife; Clarke draws the dynastic point that all these marriages kept the blood royal within the house of David. The genealogical knots are worth untying because they show the chronicler's care to trace Rehoboam's line back, through both father and mother, into the family of David — the house to which, for all the king's failings, the covenant and the throne belonged.
19:
"Which bare him children; Jeush, and Shemariah, and Zaham." The verse names three sons, and the witnesses, though few here, attend to who bore them and to the obscurity of the names. The reformed John Gill reads the opening as "and she bore him," and takes it to refer to the wife last mentioned — for it is said only of one of Rehoboam's wives that she bore, the other perhaps having died childless. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, weighing the same clause, makes Mahalath the subject: it is she, the daughter of Jerimoth and Abihail, who bore these three; and he notes that the name is more properly Shemaryahu, and that all three sons occur nowhere else in Scripture, mere names in a genealogy known only from this verse.

Keil and Delitzsch, of the same grammatical-historical school, simply set the three sons in their place — Jeush, Shamariah, and Zaham — before passing on to Rehoboam's more famous second wife and her children. There is, between Gill and Ellicott, a small difference over the antecedent of "she": Gill inclines to Abihail, the mother named just before, while Ellicott fixes on Mahalath, the wife herself; but the point is slight, for on either reading the children are Rehoboam's by his first, Davidic marriage, and the line is the same.

The witnesses do not contend, and the very thinness of the notice repays a moment's thought. These three sons, named once and never again, stand for the great multitude of Rehoboam's household that the chapter is about to number — eight and twenty sons and threescore daughters — of whom Scripture preserves only a handful of names and one who would reign. The chronicler is not writing the annals of a family for its own sake; he is tracing the survival of the house of David through a king who married back into that house and begot an heir within it. Jeush and Shemariah and Zaham are the unremembered brothers of a remembered line, the obscure sons of the first wife set down faithfully before the record turns to Maachah and to Abijah, through whom the promise to David would run. Gill and Ellicott fix the mother and correct the spelling; Keil carries the list forward. Between them they let the verse do its quiet genealogical service, numbering the children of a Davidic marriage as the first fruits of a household that the next verses will show to be, like Solomon's before it, both fruitful and dangerously large.
20:
"And after her he took Maachah the daughter of Absalom; which bare him Abijah, and Attai, and Ziza, and Shelomith." The second and best-loved wife raises a genealogical puzzle, and the witnesses work at it together. The reformed John Gill notes that some take this Absalom for David's son, but that Absalom had only one daughter, Tamar, and that the name here differs from his, being properly Abishalom, as at 1 Kings 15:2. The grammatical-historical witnesses resolve the difficulty by degrees of descent. Keil and Delitzsch reason that Maachah must be Absalom's granddaughter, not his daughter, since Absalom had but one daughter, Tamar, at 2 Samuel 14:27, and left no son at 2 Samuel 18:18; Maachah is therefore the daughter of Tamar, who according to 2 Chronicles 13:2 was married to Uriel of Gibeah. Charles Ellicott agrees, citing Josephus that Maachah was the daughter of Tamar, and gathering the cross-references at 2 Samuel 18:18 and 1 Kings 15:10.

This descent through Tamar and Uriel also unties a second knot, for the same woman is called at 2 Chronicles 13:2 "Michaiah the daughter of Uriel of Gibeah." The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the harmonization and adds the Targum's own: Maachah, being an upright woman, had her name changed to the more excellent Michaiah, and her father's named as Uriel of Gibeah, that the name of Absalom might not be remembered. Ellicott notes that her firstborn Abijah is the king called Abijam in the book of Kings, and that of the other three sons — Attai, Ziza, and Shelomith — nothing further is known.

The witnesses do not finally divide; they converge on a single reconstruction. Gill flags the improbability of a literal daughter of Absalom; Keil and Ellicott, with Josephus, make her his granddaughter through Tamar and Uriel, which reconciles the "daughter of Absalom" here with the "daughter of Uriel" elsewhere; Clarke adds the Targum's pious gloss on the change of name. Between them they show that the chronicler's genealogies, far from contradicting the older records, dovetail with them once the generations are rightly counted — and that Rehoboam's favourite wife carried in her veins the blood of both David and, through Absalom, of the tragic Tamar, so that the heir she bore stood doubly within the royal line. The knot the verse ties is not a flaw but a witness to the density of the house of David, every strand of which the chronicler is at pains to trace.
21:
"And Rehoboam loved Maachah the daughter of Absalom above all his wives and his concubines." The verse opens on the king's affection and then discloses the scale of his harem, and the witnesses weigh both. The reformed John Gill notes that this best-loved wife is the same called elsewhere Michaiah the daughter of Uriel. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set out the numbers the verse supplies: Rehoboam took eighteen wives and threescore concubines, and begat twenty-eight sons and threescore daughters, though Josephus reckons the concubines at thirty; and they observe soberly that in this Rehoboam trod in his father Solomon's footsteps, a following not altogether to be praised.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke does the arithmetic and passes a measured judgment: eighteen wives and sixty concubines, with their issue of twenty-eight sons and sixty daughters, made eighty-eight children in all — a household to whose education the king could pay but little attention. Rehoboam was "bad enough," Clarke allows, "but not so abandoned as his father"; yet numerous families are often neglected, and children by many mothers are in a worse case still. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, adds his philologist's notes: Rehoboam "loved" Maachah, who probably inherited the beauty of her mother Tamar and her grandfather Absalom; the verb rendered "he took" is a later usage for the older word, again at 2 Chronicles 13:21; and he finds the round "threescore concubines" a little suspicious, both because Josephus gives thirty and because the same number recurs at once in the "threescore daughters."

The witnesses do not divide, and their agreement carries a quiet warning. Gill identifies the favourite; Keil and Ellicott number the wives and mark the echo of Solomon; Clarke reckons the children and pities their neglect. Between them they show a king who, having married wisely back into the house of David, married also too much and too widely — measuring his royalty, as his father had, by the size of his harem. The chronicler does not moralize openly, but the witnesses hear the note beneath the numbers: a household of eighty-eight children, ruled by a father's preference for one wife above the rest, is a household sown with the seeds of rivalry, and it is precisely to forestall that rivalry, as the next verses show, that Rehoboam will have to take anxious thought for the succession. The love of Maachah that opens the verse becomes the policy of Abijah that closes the chapter.
22:
"And Rehoboam made Abijah the son of Maachah the chief, to be ruler among his brethren: for he thought to make him king." The witnesses read the verse as the king's provision for the succession, and dispute, mildly, whether it broke the law of the firstborn. The reformed John Gill takes Abijah to be made chief and head over all his sons, as the son of the most beloved wife, her firstborn, and perhaps of greater capacity than the rest. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read "made him chief, to be ruler among his brethren" as meaning that Rehoboam appointed Abijah prince — the Hebrew nagid — over the others, with the further purpose, loosely joined in the swiftness of the sentence, of making him king. Charles Ellicott, of the same school, likewise renders "appointed Abijah for head — to wit, for prince — among his brethren," the following clause explaining what "head" means: "for he thought to make him king."

Here the witnesses part company over the law. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke holds that Abijah was certainly not Rehoboam's firstborn, and that in preferring the son of his best-loved wife the king did what Deuteronomy 21:16 forbids — though he had a sort of precedent in the preference David himself showed to Solomon. Ellicott flatly denies any breach: it does not appear that Rehoboam infringed the law of Deuteronomy 21:15-17 at all, as the Speaker's Commentary had suggested, for the right of the firstborn was only a double share of a man's property, not a claim to the throne — and for the choice of a younger son to reign there was the plain precedent of 1 Kings 1:35. So the debate is real: did Rehoboam's designation of Abijah violate the Mosaic law of inheritance, excused only by David's example, or did that law never touch the royal succession in the first place? The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley leaves the point untouched, reading the verse simply: Rehoboam declared Abijah his successor and gave him dominion over his brethren.

The witnesses do not divide over the fact, only over its legality. Gill, Keil, Ellicott, and Wesley agree that Rehoboam singled out Maachah's son, made him prince among the many brothers, and purposed the crown for him; Clarke and Ellicott differ on whether the law of the firstborn was thereby overridden. Between them they show a king thinking, as Solomon's father had thought, about which of many sons should reign — and choosing, as David chose, not the eldest but the son of the wife he loved. The choice would stand: Abijah, whatever the law of the double portion, would indeed succeed to the throne of Judah.
23:
The chapter ends on the king's shrewd management of his large family — "And he dealt wisely, and dispersed of all his children throughout all the countries of Judah and Benjamin, unto every fenced city." The reformed John Gill weighs the opening verb three ways: it may mean that Rehoboam acted prudently in disposing of his sons through the kingdom; or that he "made Abijah his son to understand," taking care of his education and instruction in the art of government; or that Abijah himself "understood," having more wisdom than the rest — though Gill thinks it best interpreted of Rehoboam. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read it of the king's policy: he dispersed all his sons through the fenced cities of Judah and Benjamin, so that they were separated from one another and settled as governors, provided with abundant maintenance and with wives, and thus kept in a state of contentment.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sees a double prudence in the scattering: it was sound policy to disperse his own sons through the provinces, where they were not likely to form leagues with Jeroboam against their father; and by seeking many wives for them he strengthened his political connections, though — Clarke is careful to add — we do not find that Rehoboam formed any of the heathenish alliances into which his father had fallen. John Wesley reads the dispersal with an eye to the succession: lest his other sons should after his death unite together against Abijah, Rehoboam wisely scattered them into distant places. Charles Ellicott, of the grammatical-historical school, gathers the whole into a portrait of political sagacity: by giving each son an independent position and royal establishment, assigning him a district bound to supply his wants after the manner of the later Persian kings, the king secured their contentment and obviated both quarrels for precedence and intrigues against his destined heir; and the closing note that he "desired many wives" for them Ellicott takes as one more mark of the princely state he conferred, a numerous harem being a sign of royalty, comparing the like provision at 2 Chronicles 21:2-3.

The witnesses do not divide. Gill canvasses the senses of "dealt wisely" and settles on the king; Keil, Clarke, Wesley, and Ellicott agree on the policy and its aim — sons dispersed, provisioned, married, and so kept content and apart, that none might threaten Abijah's throne. Between them they close the chapter as it began, with a Rehoboam who has learned prudence: forbidden to hold the north by war, he holds the south by wise administration, and having chosen his heir, he takes pains that no rival brother shall undo the choice. It is the last note of a reign that, in these three good years, was strong — a strength the next chapter will show him beginning to throw away.
